questions.stem: In the SB5-MAI change mini-process, which phase is described as the last phase, whose concrete activities depend on the specific software process?
questions.explanation: Conclusion is the last phase of a software change and the deck states explicitly that its activities depend on the specific software process. Verification is a band wrapping every phase, not the last phase. Actualization is where the change is implemented (earlier). Postfactoring is refactoring after actualization, which precedes Conclusion. (Conclusion p.1)

questions.stem: What are the three steps of the conclusion phase, in order?
questions.explanation: The conclusion proceeds commit (return code to the team's CM repository) -> new baseline (certify the integrated whole) -> new release (deliver to users). 'New release, new baseline, commit' reverses the order. 'Baseline, commit, release' swaps commit and baseline. The acceptance-test/patch option substitutes those for baseline/release as the named steps. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: During the commit step of conclusion, where do programmers return their updated code?
questions.explanation: Commit means returning updated code to the configuration-management repository while resolving conflicts with what others committed in the meantime. A local branch is not the shared repository the commit targets. The acceptance environment is for stakeholder testing, not commit. The release server receives released code, which is a later step. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: Why does the commit step of conclusion involve resolving conflicts?

options.text: Because others may have committed changes to the same repository in the meantime
Because the merge tool corrupts patches
Because the baseline test rewrote the code
Because stakeholders edited the code during acceptance testing

questions.explanation: Commit returns code to the shared CM repository, and conflicts arise because other programmers may have committed overlapping changes since the developer branched. The baseline test certifies, it does not rewrite source. Stakeholders run acceptance tests, they do not edit code. The merge tool incorporates patches into users' programs, it does not corrupt them. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: Using the 'widening blast radius' mental model, the three conclusion steps hand the code to which audiences respectively?
questions.explanation: The guide frames commit as handing code to the team, baseline as certifying it for the project, and release as shipping it to users - each a wider blast radius and higher bar. The other orderings scramble this progression. (Conclusion p.2, p.9)

questions.stem: Why does the deck refuse to give a single recipe for the conclusion's activities?

options.text: Because the activities are identical to actualization
Because the precise activities depend on the specific software process
Because conclusion is optional and often skipped
Because conclusion is performed only by stakeholders

questions.explanation: The Conclusion deck is explicit that the precise activities depend on the specific software process (a continuous-integration shop concludes differently from a quarterly-release shop). Conclusion is not optional. It is not performed solely by stakeholders (programmers commit, the testing team baselines). It is not identical to actualization. (Conclusion p.1)

questions.stem: A continuous-integration shop and a quarterly-release shop conclude a change differently. What does the deck say nonetheless stays the same?

options.text: The same three logical steps (commit, baseline, release) still occur

questions.explanation: Although the precise activities depend on the software process, the same three logical steps occur in every shop. The tooling, cadence, stakeholder count, and release sizes all vary by process, so they are not the invariant. (Conclusion p.1)

questions.stem: What is a baseline in the conclusion phase?

options.text: A tested, agreed-upon version of the whole system the team builds on
A single developer's uncommitted local copy
The list of open bug reports

questions.explanation: A baseline is a thoroughly-tested, agreed-upon whole-system version representing progress. A developer's local copy is not yet agreed/tested. The bug-report stack is where deferred fixes go, not the baseline. The record/playback script is acceptance-test machinery. (Conclusion p.2-3)

questions.stem: The two guarantees that baseline testing must provide are that the new baseline is as bug-free as possible and that it represents what?

options.text: A complete rewrite of the prior version
Progress of the project, not a regression

questions.explanation: Baseline testing guarantees the baseline is as bug-free as possible AND that it is progress, not a regression - it must never be worse than the previous baseline on already-working functionality. It is not a rewrite, a coverage reduction, or a build-time goal. (Conclusion p.3)

questions.stem: Which best captures the 'not a regression' requirement of a new baseline?

options.text: All previous tests must be deleted
Already-working functionality must not become worse than in the previous baseline
The baseline must ship to users immediately
Every feature must be rewritten from scratch

questions.explanation: Not-a-regression means the baseline is never worse than its predecessor on functionality that already worked. It does not demand rewrites, test deletion, or immediate release (release is a separate, later step). (Conclusion p.3)

questions.stem: Baseline testing is described as expensive. Which detail does the deck give about how it is run?

options.text: Performed only by the original author of the change
Often done overnight or over the weekend by a specialised testing team
Done in seconds inside the developer's IDE

questions.explanation: Baseline testing can take significant time, is often run overnight or over the weekend, and is conducted by a specialised testing team. It is not an instant IDE action, not done solely by the change's author, and not skipped for small changes. (Conclusion p.3)

questions.stem: Postponing baseline testing too long has which downside?

options.text: Bugs accumulate, making the eventual testing task harder

questions.explanation: If baseline testing is postponed too long, bugs accumulate and the eventual testing job is harder. Build speed, the merge tool, and commit access are unrelated to baseline frequency. (Conclusion p.4)

questions.stem: Running baseline testing too frequently produces which downside?
questions.explanation: Testing too frequently creates unnecessary overhead. Accumulated bugs are the opposite failure mode (testing too rarely). Frequency does not delete regression tests, and frequent testing does not by definition break the baseline. (Conclusion p.4)

questions.stem: What drives the choice of how frequently to create a new baseline?

options.text: The colour code on the class diagram
The number of stakeholders at acceptance
The number of figure types in the system

questions.explanation: Baseline frequency is a tuning decision driven by program size and required quality. Figure-type count and diagram colour codes belong to the Drawlets example, not the frequency decision; stakeholder count drives acceptance testing scope, not baseline cadence. (Conclusion p.4)

questions.stem: The moment baseline testing starts functions as what for programmers?
questions.explanation: The start of baseline testing is a hard deadline to commit. It is not a code review, not the user release date (release is a later step), and not stakeholder acceptance sign-off. (Conclusion p.5)

questions.stem: A programmer misses the baseline deadline. What is the immediate consequence for their work?

options.text: It is released to users as a patch
It is automatically merged into the current baseline anyway
It is not in this baseline; they must submit by the next deadline

questions.explanation: Missing the deadline means the work is not in this baseline and must be submitted by the next one. It is not auto-merged, not discarded, and not released - missing the gate excludes it from the current baseline. (Conclusion p.5)

questions.stem: Beyond simply waiting for the next baseline, what extra cost does missing the deadline impose?

options.text: Immediate invalidation of the whole increment
A mandatory rewrite of the change request
Additional work to re-integrate against a now-moved baseline
Loss of the developer's commit rights

questions.explanation: Re-submitting at the next deadline means extra, sometimes significant, work to re-integrate against a baseline that has moved on. It does not force a request rewrite or revoke commit rights. Increment invalidation is the consequence of a broken baseline, not a missed deadline. (Conclusion p.5)

questions.stem: How does the baseline deadline act as a social/accountability mechanism?

options.text: Management knows how often a programmer missed it and may require an explanation

questions.explanation: Management knows how often a particular programmer missed the deadline and may require an explanation, making the baseline both a technical sync point and an accountability mechanism. The deck does not mention salaries, automatic demotion, or branch deletion. (Conclusion p.5)

questions.stem: When do bugs in committed code typically surface in the conclusion phase?
questions.explanation: Faulty committed code reveals its bugs during baseline testing. Concept location, impact analysis, and prefactoring all precede commit, so the bugs are not surfaced there. (Conclusion p.6)

questions.stem: In the 'broken baseline' (serious) case, what does the testing team do?

options.text: Hand the bugs to stakeholders to fix
Certify the baseline and defer fixes to the bug stack
Reject the buggy commits; the whole increment may be invalidated or postponed
Silently merge the bugs and continue

questions.explanation: A broken baseline means the testing team rejects the buggy commits, and sometimes the whole increment's work is invalidated or postponed - a significant cost on large projects. Certify-and-defer is the MINOR (bugs-in-baseline) response, not this one. They do not silently merge or delegate fixes to stakeholders. (Conclusion p.7)

questions.stem: A serious defect causes the testing team to reject the increment so that no new baseline is created. Which case is this, and what is its distinguishing consequence?

options.text: Bugs in baseline; fixes are deferred to the bug stack
Broken baseline; the increment's work is invalidated/postponed and reputations suffer
Missed deadline; the work waits for the next baseline

questions.explanation: No-new-baseline plus invalidated increment plus reputational harm is the broken-baseline outcome. Bugs-in-baseline would still certify and defer. A missed deadline is about timing, not defect severity. Acceptance failure is a stakeholder-side, post-baseline event. (Conclusion p.7)

questions.stem: Two commits each contain a defect. Commit X's defect is minor and the team certifies the baseline anyway; commit Y's defect is serious and the increment is rejected. What single attribute determines which path is taken?

options.text: Whether the change had a prefactoring step
The severity of the bug, judged by the testing team

questions.explanation: The bugs-in-baseline vs. broken-baseline distinction is a severity judgement made by the testing team. Commit order, LOC count, and presence of prefactoring do not decide whether the baseline is certified or rejected. (Conclusion p.6-7)

questions.stem: After a broken baseline, what additional consequence can fall on individual programmers?

options.text: They are immediately reassigned to a different project
The team can identify who committed buggy files and their reputation suffers
They lose access to the bug-report stack

questions.explanation: On a broken baseline the team can identify the programmers who committed buggy files and their reputation suffers. The deck does not mention reassignment, loss of bug-stack access, or deletion of prior baselines. (Conclusion p.7)

questions.stem: Which statement correctly contrasts the two baseline-defect cases?

options.text: Bugs-in-baseline certifies and defers fixes; broken-baseline rejects commits and may invalidate the increment
Both cases certify the baseline; only the reputation effect differs
Bugs-in-baseline invalidates the increment; broken-baseline defers fixes
Both cases reject the commits; only the cause differs

questions.explanation: Bugs-in-baseline = certify + defer-to-stack; broken-baseline = reject + possibly invalidate. The other options invert the responses or wrongly claim both reject or both certify. (Conclusion p.6-7)

questions.stem: Who conducts acceptance testing in the conclusion phase?
questions.explanation: Acceptance testing is functional testing done by the stakeholders. The specialised testing team certifies the internal baseline; acceptance is the external stakeholder gate. It is not the author alone or an automated CM tool. (Conclusion p.8)

questions.stem: Acceptance testing serves two purposes in the conclusion. What are they?

options.text: Resolves merge conflicts AND creates patches
Gives stakeholders progress information AND is the gate to approve software for release
Replaces baseline testing AND deletes obsolete tests

questions.explanation: Acceptance testing both informs stakeholders of progress and is the gate by which they approve software for release. It does not replace baseline testing, estimate impact, classify concepts, or resolve merge conflicts. (Conclusion p.8)

questions.stem: Acceptance testing is best described as which kind of testing?

options.text: Unit testing done by the developers
Mutation testing done by the CI server
Functional testing done by the stakeholders

questions.explanation: Acceptance testing is functional testing done by the stakeholders that thoroughly exercises software functionalities. It is not developer unit testing, ops performance testing, or CI mutation testing. (Conclusion p.8)

questions.stem: What does a release deliver, and to whom?

options.text: Uncommitted code, to the testing team

questions.explanation: A release delivers baseline code to users. It is not uncommitted code, bug reports, or concept classifications, and its audience is users, not the testing team. (Conclusion p.9)

questions.stem: Release frequency is characterised as which kind of decision?

options.text: A decision made only by the merge tool
Both a technical and a business decision

questions.explanation: The deck says release requires substantial extra work and its frequency is both a technical and a business decision. It is not purely technical, not legal, and not made by tooling. (Conclusion p.9)

questions.stem: The 'versioned model of software lifespan' (e.g. 'AwesomeApp 4.2') refers to what release pattern?

options.text: Mixing infrequent large releases with frequent small releases
Releasing a brand-new product every quarter
Never releasing until the project ends

questions.explanation: The versioned model mixes less-frequent large releases with more-frequent small releases, captured by version numbers like AwesomeApp 4.2. It is not a new product each quarter, not release-at-end-only, and not patches-only. (Conclusion p.9)

questions.stem: How are large releases delivered to users?

options.text: Merged into the running program by the tool 'merge'

questions.explanation: Large releases are downloaded and installed. Merging via the 'merge' tool is how SMALL releases (patches) are delivered. Streaming during baseline testing and verbal approval are not delivery mechanisms. (Conclusion p.10)

questions.stem: Which row correctly matches release size to delivery method?

options.text: Large = verbal approval; small = full reinstall
Large = patch merged; small = downloaded and installed
Large = downloaded and installed; small = patch merged via 'merge'
Large = bug stack; small = regression test

questions.explanation: Large releases are downloaded/installed; small releases are patches merged via 'merge'. The 'large=patch merged, small=installed' option swaps them. The remaining options pair release sizes with unrelated concepts (verbal approval/reinstall, bug stack/regression test). (Conclusion p.10)

questions.stem: Why does a release require 'substantial extra work' beyond creating a baseline?

options.text: Because stakeholders must re-run concept location
Delivering to actual users involves packaging, installation/patching, and business coordination
Because the baseline must be rewritten for each user
Because the bug stack must be emptied first

questions.explanation: Release adds substantial work because the baseline must be packaged and delivered to real users (install or patch) and coordinated as a business decision. The baseline is not rewritten per user, concept location is not re-run, and the bug stack need not be emptied. (Conclusion p.9-10)

questions.stem: A developer says 'I committed, so the change is done.' Why is this wrong?

options.text: Commit automatically creates a patch for users
Commit is the final step and they are correct
Commit only hands code to the team; it must still pass baseline testing and be released

questions.explanation: Commit is one of three steps and only hands code to the team; the code must still pass baseline testing and (for users) be released, each a wider gate. Commit does not include acceptance and does not auto-create a user patch. (Conclusion p.2, p.8-9)

questions.stem: Which statement about the conclusion phase is the single most accurate?

options.text: The conclusion is just committing to Git
Commit, baseline certification, stakeholder acceptance, and release are distinct gates of increasing scope
Baseline testing and acceptance testing are performed by the same team

questions.explanation: The conclusion is a sequence of widening gates: commit (team) -> baseline (project, testing team) -> acceptance (stakeholders) -> release (users). It is not 'just committing'. Baseline testing (testing team) and acceptance testing (stakeholders) are different actors. Release follows, not precedes, certification. (Conclusion p.2-10)

questions.stem: Which sequence of internal-then-external quality gates is correct for the conclusion?

options.text: Both are external and run by stakeholders
Both are internal and run simultaneously

questions.explanation: Baseline testing is the internal certification by the testing team; acceptance testing is the external gate by stakeholders, and it follows. The other options swap the roles, conflate them, or wrongly make both external. (Conclusion p.3, p.8)

questions.stem: What kind of software is Drawlets?

options.text: An application framework that adds a drawing canvas to a host application

questions.explanation: Drawlets is an application framework that adds a graphical drawing canvas to a host application. It is not a database, a CI server, or a linter. (Drawlets p.1)

questions.stem: Who originally created Drawlets?

options.text: Erich Gamma and the Gang of Four

questions.explanation: Drawlets was originally written by Kent Beck and Ward Cunningham, later ported to Java. The GoF wrote Design Patterns, Martin wrote Clean Code/Architecture, and Fowler wrote Refactoring - all cited readings, not Drawlets' authors. (Drawlets p.2)

questions.stem: Drawlets is marketed as a what?
questions.explanation: Drawlets, ported to Java, was marketed as a 'perfect API'. The other slogans do not appear in the deck. (Drawlets p.2)

questions.stem: What is the name of the bundled host application that ships with Drawlets?
questions.explanation: SimpleApplet is the bundled host application; it provides the canvas, toolbars and buttons and runs in any browser. SimpleDrawingCanvas is the canvas implementation, SimpleListener is a new class added by the change, and AbstractFigure holds figure properties. (Drawlets p.3)

questions.stem: Where does SimpleApplet run?
questions.explanation: SimpleApplet runs in any browser. It is not server-only, CI-only, or emulator-only. (Drawlets p.3)

questions.stem: In the SimpleApplet window, which control is specifically called out alongside Fill/Line/Text/Back/Fore/Apply?
questions.explanation: The SimpleApplet window shows style controls (Fill/Line/Text/Back/Fore/Apply) plus an ID button down the right. Merge, baseline, and backtrack are concepts from elsewhere in the course, not buttons in that window. (Drawlets p.4)

questions.stem: On the 'top classes' diagram, which four classes are highlighted as reachable straight from the host, anchoring where comprehension starts?
questions.explanation: Slide 5 highlights StylePalette, SimpleApplet, ToolBar and ToolPalette as host-reachable, anchoring comprehension. The figure hierarchy, the leaf tools, and the method names are not the highlighted host-reachable classes. (Drawlets p.5)

questions.stem: Why are both JHotDraw and Drawlets used to teach software maintenance?

options.text: Both are large monolithic scripts with no structure
Both are well-patterned OO drawing frameworks whose structure makes propagation paths legible
Both are databases with stored procedures

questions.explanation: Both are well-patterned object-oriented drawing frameworks (Figure abstraction, canvas, tools), and good patterning makes propagation paths legible - ideal for teaching maintenance. They are not unstructured scripts, databases, or test-free. (Drawlets p.2, JHotDraw connection)

questions.stem: Which design patterns does the guide explicitly associate with Drawlets' structure?
questions.explanation: The guide names Composite (figures), Strategy (tools), and Observer/listeners - the new SimpleListener being an Observer addition. The other pattern sets are not the ones cited for Drawlets. (Drawlets p.16, JHotDraw connection)

questions.stem: What is the relationship between Drawlets and JHotDraw in this lecture?

options.text: Drawlets is the sibling case study used to trace one change through all eight phases in sequence
Drawlets is a refactored version of JHotDraw
They are unrelated; only one is a drawing framework
JHotDraw is a plugin for Drawlets

questions.explanation: Drawlets is JHotDraw's sibling drawing framework, used to walk one change through all eight phases on a single request, whereas JHotDraw teaches the phases in isolation. Neither is a refactor or plugin of the other, and both are drawing frameworks. (Drawlets, JHotDraw connection)

questions.stem: What is the Drawlets change request?

options.text: Replace the canvas with a 3D renderer
Add a new triangle figure type
Migrate the framework from Java to Kotlin
Implement an owner for each figure so only the owner may modify it

questions.explanation: The change request is to implement an owner per figure - the user who placed it - so only the owner may modify it, with ID/password entered at session start. New figure types, a 3D renderer, and a language migration are not the request. (Drawlets p.6)

questions.stem: In the owner change, when do users become owners of figures?

options.text: At session start they input ID and password, becoming owners of all figures created during that session
Only when the baseline is certified

questions.explanation: Users input their ID and password at the beginning of a session and become owners of all figures created during that session. Owner assignment is not tied to acceptance, baseline certification, or release downloads. (Drawlets p.6)

questions.stem: What does the Initiation phase establish for the owner change?

options.text: That a worth-doing change request exists
Which exact class will hold the owner
The number of unit tests to write

questions.explanation: Initiation means a request exists and is deemed worth doing. Locating the class is concept location, the test count is verification, and baseline pass/fail is conclusion - all later. (Drawlets p.6)

questions.stem: The first step of concept location on the request is to do what with its wording?

options.text: Extract the concepts (nouns/verbs) that might map to code

questions.explanation: Concept location begins by extracting concepts - the nouns and verbs (owner, figure, canvas, session, ID, ...) - that might map to code. Editing, testing, and committing come much later. (Drawlets p.7)

questions.stem: Concept classification sorts each extracted concept into which three buckets?
questions.explanation: Concepts are classified into irrelevant, external, and significant. Difficulty levels, diagram colours, and conclusion steps are unrelated category sets. (Drawlets p.8)

questions.stem: Which concepts are classified as 'significant' (already in the design, central to the change)?
questions.explanation: figure and canvas are the significant concepts - already in the design. owner/ID/password are external; implement/modify are irrelevant generic English. (Drawlets p.8)

questions.stem: Which concepts are classified as 'external' (crossing the system boundary, coming from outside)?
questions.explanation: owner, ID and password are external concepts that come from outside the system boundary. figure/canvas are significant, implement/modify/created are irrelevant, and Figure/AbstractFigure/SimpleApplet are code classes, not extracted English concepts. (Drawlets p.8)

questions.stem: Which of these is classified as 'irrelevant' in the Drawlets concept table?
questions.explanation: implement (along with user, allowed, modify, beginning, input, created) is irrelevant - generic English, not a code concept. figure/canvas are significant; owner is external. (Drawlets p.8)

questions.stem: Why does concept classification matter before searching the code?

options.text: It tells you which concept (the significant 'figure') to anchor the search on, not 'owner'
It selects which unit tests to delete

questions.explanation: Classification identifies the significant concept (figure) to anchor the search; owner is external and is what you ADD, not where you search. It has nothing to do with release cadence, test deletion, or the deadline. (Drawlets p.8)

questions.stem: A student anchors the concept-location search on 'owner' rather than 'figure'. Per the guide, why is this the wrong anchor?

options.text: 'owner' is already a class, so no search is needed
'owner' is the canvas, so the search is redundant
'owner' is irrelevant English with no meaning
'owner' is an external concept being added; the significant existing concept 'figure' is where to search

questions.explanation: owner is classified external - it crosses the boundary and is what the change introduces - so you search on the significant existing concept figure (its class holds the properties owner must attach to). owner is not irrelevant, not the canvas, and not yet a class. (Drawlets p.8)

questions.stem: On Drawlets, concept location is presented as which kind of activity?

options.text: A search over the class dependency graph that can go wrong and backtrack

questions.explanation: Concept location is a search over the class dependency graph that can take a wrong turn and backtrack - search, not lookup. It is not a dictionary lookup, a type check, or an acceptance test. (Drawlets p.9-15)

questions.stem: Where does the Drawlets concept-location search start?
questions.explanation: The search starts at SimpleApplet, the already-comprehended host, asking where a figure's properties live. AbstractFigure is the eventual ANSWER, not the start; the bug stack and leaf tools are unrelated to where comprehension begins. (Drawlets p.9)

questions.stem: Why is SimpleDrawingCanvas the wrong place for a figure's owner property?

options.text: The canvas holds figures but is not where a single figure's own properties belong
The canvas is external to the system
The canvas is a new class added by the change

questions.explanation: SimpleDrawingCanvas is a container that holds figures, but a single figure's own property (its owner) belongs on the figure abstraction, not the container. The canvas is not new, not dependency-free, and not external. (Drawlets p.10, p.13)

questions.stem: What does 'backtrack' mean in the Drawlets concept-location walk?

options.text: Mark the dead-end branch abandoned and return to the last good class

questions.explanation: Backtracking abandons the dead-end candidate (it greys out) and returns to the last good class to try another path. It is not a Git undo, a re-baseline, or a re-classification. (Drawlets p.11, p.14)

questions.stem: Which class does the Summary slide mark (in red) as the concept-location result for 'figure properties'?
questions.explanation: The Summary slide marks AbstractFigure in red as the class holding figure properties, so the owner goes there. SimpleDrawingCanvas was the rejected wrong way; SimpleApplet was the start; ToolPalette was greyed out. (Drawlets p.15)

questions.stem: A maintainer hypothesises figure properties live in SimpleDrawingCanvas, examines it, rejects it, greys it out, and returns to follow Figure up to AbstractFigure. Which two concept-location ideas does this sequence illustrate?

options.text: A wrong hypothesis tested against code, then backtracking to the correct abstraction
Impact analysis followed by change propagation

questions.explanation: Examining-and-rejecting a candidate then returning to try the abstraction is exactly hypothesis-test-then-backtrack within concept location. It is not impact analysis/propagation, not pre/postfactoring, and not commit/baseline - those are other phases. (Drawlets p.10-15)

questions.stem: With AbstractFigure as the change origin, what does impact analysis estimate?

options.text: The final number of LOC typed
Which other classes will be touched (the blast radius)

questions.explanation: Impact analysis estimates the blast radius - which other classes the change will touch - before coding. Final LOC, release cadence, and acceptance results are not impact-analysis outputs. (Drawlets p.16, Phase 3)

questions.stem: Impact analysis predicts the owner change will ripple to roughly which part of Drawlets?

options.text: The whole upper graph: tool tree, canvas/sequence, palettes, and SimpleApplet

questions.explanation: Because many classes create, hold, or move figures, impact analysis predicts the ripple reaches the tool tree, the canvas/sequence, the palettes, and SimpleApplet - roughly the whole upper graph. It is not confined to AbstractFigure, the new classes, or test files. (Drawlets p.16, Phase 3)

questions.stem: What is the practical value of impact analysis before coding the owner change?

options.text: It signals this is a non-trivial, cross-cutting change rather than a one-class edit
It resolves merge conflicts in advance
It writes the new unit tests automatically

questions.explanation: Impact analysis tells you in advance that the change is cross-cutting (many classes), not a single-class edit - guiding planning. It does not write tests, resolve conflicts, or certify baselines. (Drawlets p.16, Phase 3)

questions.stem: Impact analysis predicted a wide ripple. How was its estimate confirmed retrospectively?

options.text: By actualization modifying ~13 classes and adding 2 new ones

questions.explanation: The wide-ripple estimate was confirmed retrospectively by actualization modifying about 13 classes and adding 2 new ones. Test pass counts, baseline certification, and acceptance approval do not measure the predicted blast radius. (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: Why does an owner-check added to figure modification necessarily ripple to the tool tree and containers?

options.text: Every tool that modifies figures and every container that stores them is affected by the owner constraint
The owner-check is placed in SimpleApplet only
Tools and containers are external concepts

questions.explanation: Because every tool that modifies figures and every container that stores them interacts with the now-owner-constrained figure, the change reaches the tool tree and canvas/sequence. Tools/containers are internal, the check is not SimpleApplet-only, and containers do reference figures. (Drawlets p.16, Phase 3)

questions.stem: Did the Drawlets walkthrough perform a separate prefactoring step before actualization?

options.text: No - it went straight to actualization; prefactoring was deemed unnecessary here

questions.explanation: The deck performs no separate prefactoring step; it goes straight to actualization, deeming prefactoring unnecessary for this change. Extracting moveFigure and splitting move/secureMove are the POST-factoring refactorings, studied after. Prefactoring is optional, not forbidden. (Drawlets p.31-38, Phase 4)

questions.stem: The guide notes that the closing refactoring study shows exactly what prefactoring one WOULD do. What are those two clean-ups?

options.text: Move moveFigure up to ConstructionTool, and split move/secureMove
Delete obsolete tests and add new ones

questions.explanation: The closing study shows the prefactoring one would do: pull moveFigure up into ConstructionTool and split AbstractFigure.move into move/secureMove. Test maintenance, conclusion steps, and concept-location steps are different activities. (Drawlets p.31-36)

questions.stem: How does the guide characterise prefactoring as a phase, given the Drawlets example?

options.text: Optional - performed only when it makes the change cheaper

questions.explanation: Prefactoring is optional and was skipped here because it was unnecessary. It is not mandatory, not stakeholder work, and not baseline testing. (Drawlets, Phase 4)

questions.stem: Which two new classes are added during actualization of the owner change?
questions.explanation: Actualization adds OwnerIdentity (the user identity an owner carries) and SimpleListener (an Observer wiring it in). AbstractFigure/Figure already existed, move/secureMove are methods, and RectangleTool/EllipseTool are existing leaf tools. (Drawlets p.16)

questions.stem: What role does OwnerIdentity play in the actualized design?

options.text: It is the user identity that an owner carries
It is the canvas that holds figures
It is the base class for creation tools
It is the Observer listener wiring

questions.explanation: OwnerIdentity is the user identity an owner carries. The canvas is SimpleDrawingCanvas, the Observer wiring is SimpleListener, and the creation-tool base is ConstructionTool. (Drawlets p.16)

questions.stem: What design-pattern role does the new SimpleListener class play?

options.text: An Observer/listener wiring the identity in

questions.explanation: SimpleListener is an Observer/listener that wires the identity in. Composite describes figures, Strategy describes tools, and there is no Singleton canvas in the change. (Drawlets p.16)

questions.stem: Where does the change BEGIN during actualization?

options.text: At AbstractFigure (the located class), which gains the owner concept

questions.explanation: Actualization begins at the located class AbstractFigure (marked red), which gains the owner concept, with OwnerIdentity/SimpleListener introduced to support it. It does not begin at leaf tools, SimpleApplet, or the palette. (Drawlets p.16)

questions.stem: After OwnerIdentity and SimpleListener are introduced, which existing classes are shown depending on them?
questions.explanation: On the actualization slide, SimpleDrawingCanvas and AbstractFigure now depend on the new OwnerIdentity/SimpleListener. The palettes and leaf tools are reached later during propagation, not as the initial dependents. (Drawlets p.16)

questions.stem: Once one class is changed, what makes change propagation necessary?

options.text: The changed class becomes inconsistent with its neighbours, which must be visited and changed-or-confirmed

questions.explanation: A changed class becomes inconsistent with its neighbours; propagation visits each and changes-or-confirms it, possibly making ITS neighbours inconsistent, as a wave until consistency is restored. It is not about baseline failure, cadence, or re-classification. (Drawlets p.16-24)

questions.stem: When does change propagation terminate?
questions.explanation: Propagation ends at 'Propagation - done' when no inconsistencies remain and the system is internally consistent again. Release, test counts, and deadlines are not the termination condition. (Drawlets p.24)

questions.stem: In the Drawlets propagation colour code, what does a RED class mean?

options.text: A class changed / now inconsistent and needing attention
The current frontier node being worked
A new class added by the change
A class checked and confirmed unchanged

questions.explanation: Red marks a class that is changed/inconsistent and needs attention. Blue boxes are new classes, grey/green are checked-and-consistent, and orange is the current frontier. (Drawlets p.16-24)

questions.stem: In the propagation colour code, what does a GREY class mean by 'Propagation - done'?
questions.explanation: Grey marks classes that were checked but did not need changing (e.g. ToolBar, ToolPalette, CanvasTool, Locator, SequenceOfFigures). Red is changed, blue is new, orange is the frontier. (Drawlets p.24)

questions.stem: In the propagation diagrams, what does an ORANGE node represent?

options.text: The current frontier node being worked

questions.explanation: Orange is the current frontier node being worked. New classes are blue boxes, confirmed-unchanged are grey, and external concepts belong to concept classification, not the propagation colours. (Drawlets p.16-24)

questions.stem: On 'Propagation - 3' (p.20), the palettes (StylePalette, ToolBar, ToolPalette) settle to grey. What does that signify?

options.text: They were checked and confirmed unaffected by the change
They were changed to enforce ownership
They are new classes added by the change

questions.explanation: The palettes settle to grey = confirmed unaffected. They were not changed, are not new, and are not the located concept (which was AbstractFigure). (Drawlets p.20)

questions.stem: By 'Propagation - 5' (p.22), most of the upper graph is red. What does this large red set represent?

options.text: The classes that genuinely require edits for the owner change

questions.explanation: The large red set at p.22 is the classes that genuinely require edits (LocatorConnectionHandle, StylePalette, SimpleApplet, SelectionTool, ConstructionTool, PrototypeConstructionTool, AbstractFigure, Figure). They are not deleted, not baseline failures, and not external concepts. (Drawlets p.22)

questions.stem: On 'Propagation - 6' (p.23), the frontier reaches the leaf figure tools. Which classes are these?
questions.explanation: The leaf figure tools checked at the frontier are RectangleTool, EllipseTool, RectangularCreationTool and PG_RectImageTool (the ShapeTool subtree). OwnerIdentity/SimpleListener are new classes, Figure/AbstractFigure are the abstraction, and the palettes were settled earlier. (Drawlets p.23)

questions.stem: What three categories of class are visible in the final 'Propagation - done' diagram (p.24)?

options.text: A large red set of modified classes, grey checked-but-unchanged classes, and 2 blue new classes

questions.explanation: The done diagram shows modified (red), checked-but-unchanged (grey), and the 2 new (blue) classes. It is not only red, only new, or external concepts. (Drawlets p.24)

questions.stem: What is the single most important takeaway from the Drawlets propagation animation (p.16-24)?

options.text: Propagation is the same as concept location
Propagation is a one-class edit with no neighbours affected
One edit at AbstractFigure propagated across roughly the whole upper graph, visited class-by-class

questions.explanation: The heart of the lecture: a single edit at AbstractFigure rippled across roughly the whole upper graph, each class changed (red) or confirmed (grey) until marks cleared. It is not a one-class edit, not new-classes-only, and distinct from concept location (which precedes it). (Drawlets p.16-24)

questions.stem: Which classes does the guide list as checked-but-unchanged (grey) at the end of propagation?
questions.explanation: The grey checked-but-unchanged classes are ToolBar, ToolPalette, CanvasTool, Locator and SequenceOfFigures. AbstractFigure/Figure/SimpleApplet were changed (red), OwnerIdentity/SimpleListener are new, and the leaf tools were checked at the ShapeTool subtree frontier. (Drawlets p.24)

questions.stem: What does the deck claim refactoring can do for change propagation?
questions.explanation: A long propagation is a problem, and refactoring can SHORTEN it (by moving code into fewer classes and splitting roles). It does not lengthen it, remove tests, or replace concept location. (Drawlets p.31)

questions.stem: The two refactoring strategies for shortening propagation are which pair?

options.text: Moving code into fewer classes, and splitting the roles of an overloaded method
Adding more classes, and merging all tests
Increasing baseline frequency, and adding stakeholders
Deleting the canvas, and renaming figures

questions.explanation: The two strategies are moving affected code into fewer classes and splitting the roles of an overloaded method so only one role needs updating. The other options are unrelated. (Drawlets p.31)

questions.stem: In the 'move the code' refactoring, which method contains the duplicated logic that gets extracted?

options.text: move(...), used only for user moves
basicNewFigure(...), which both creates a figure and moves it if at the wrong location

questions.explanation: basicNewFigure(...) exists in every figure-creating class and does two things: create a figure and, if at the wrong location, move it. The duplicated move logic is extracted. move/secureMove are the role-split result, and SimpleListener is the new Observer. (Drawlets p.33)

questions.stem: Which Fowler refactorings does the 'move the code' step combine?

options.text: Extract Method and Pull Up Method
Inline Method and Push Down Field
Replace Conditional with Polymorphism and Rename
Introduce Parameter Object and Encapsulate Field

questions.explanation: Moving the code combines Extract Method (pull the move logic out of basicNewFigure into moveFigure) and Pull Up Method (raise it into ConstructionTool). The other Fowler refactorings are not the ones the deck uses here. (Drawlets p.33)

questions.stem: In the 'split the roles' refactoring, what observation enables shortening the propagation?

options.text: Two different methods do the same thing and can be merged
The method is never called and can be deleted
The method is external to the system
The same method is used in two different roles, and only one role needs updating

questions.explanation: Splitting works because one method serves two roles; propagating the change highlights the difference and only one role needs updating. It is not about merging duplicate methods, deleting dead code, or external methods. (Drawlets p.35)

questions.stem: After splitting move(...) into move(...) and secureMove(...), which variant carries the owner identity check?

options.text: Neither; the check stays in basicNewFigure

questions.explanation: secureMove(...) is the user-requested move that must check user identity (owner); the plain move(...) is the creation-time move that needs no identity check and stays unchanged, shortening propagation. The check is not in plain move, both, or basicNewFigure. (Drawlets p.36)

questions.stem: Why does the creation path stay unchanged after the move/secureMove split?

options.text: Creation no longer moves figures at all
Creation is handled by SimpleListener now
Creation-time moves keep the cheap plain move(...) which needs no identity check
Creation is external to the system

questions.explanation: Creation-time moves do not need an owner check, so they keep the cheap plain move(...) and stay unchanged - the secure variant is only for user-requested moves. Creation still moves figures, is not delegated to the listener, and is not external. (Drawlets p.36)

questions.stem: Which statement best distinguishes the two postfactoring refactorings on Drawlets?

options.text: 'Move the code' relocates duplicated move logic up to ConstructionTool; 'split the roles' separates AbstractFigure.move into checked/unchecked variants
'Move the code' splits a method; 'split the roles' pulls a method up

questions.explanation: Move-the-code = Extract+Pull Up moveFigure into ConstructionTool; split-the-roles = split AbstractFigure.move into move/secureMove. They are different mechanisms; the 'move-the-code splits a method / split-the-roles pulls up' option inverts them, and the 'both move into ConstructionTool' and 'both split AbstractFigure.move' options wrongly equate them. (Drawlets p.33-36)

questions.stem: In the worked example's conclusion phase, what happens to the finished owner change?

options.text: It is committed, tested as a new baseline, and eventually released

questions.explanation: Per the Conclusion deck applied to the example, the finished change is committed (resolving conflicts), tested as a new baseline (progress-not-regression), and eventually released. It is not discarded, re-located, or split (that was postfactoring). (Conclusion p.2-10)

questions.stem: The Drawlets deck supplies the acceptance-test machinery for the conclusion. How were the acceptance tests built?

options.text: By stakeholders editing the source directly
By hand-writing assertions in JUnit only
With a record/playback tool for Java applications

questions.explanation: Acceptance tests were built with a record/playback tool for Java applications, feeding the stakeholders' acceptance decision. They were not pure hand-written JUnit, mutation tests, or source edits. (Drawlets p.27)

questions.stem: How many unit tests does the Drawlets baseline have?
questions.explanation: There are 385 unit tests. 141 is the functional-test count, 1369 is the assertion count, and 124 is the modified-test LOC. (Drawlets p.25)

questions.stem: How many assertions accompany the 385 unit tests?
questions.explanation: The 385 unit tests contain 1369 assertions. 385 is the test count, 141 is functional tests, and 4800 is the unit-test LOC. (Drawlets p.25)

questions.stem: How many lines of code make up Drawlets' unit-test suite?
questions.explanation: The unit-test code is about 4800 LOC. 17 800 is the production baseline, 40 000 is the whole framework, and 124 is the test LOC the change modified. (Drawlets p.25, p.30)

questions.stem: How many functional test cases does Drawlets have?
questions.explanation: There are 141 functional test cases (Draw, Select, Move, ...). 385 is the unit-test count, 1369 the assertions, and 13 the no-refactor classes-modified. (Drawlets p.26)

questions.stem: Which are examples of Drawlets' scenario-style functional tests?

options.text: Draw (figure drawn), Select (figure selected), Move (figure moved)

questions.explanation: Functional tests are scenario-style: Draw, Select, Move, etc. Commit/Baseline/Release are conclusion steps, Extract/PullUp/Inline are refactorings, and Irrelevant/External/Significant are concept categories. (Drawlets p.26)

questions.stem: During actualization, what three kinds of test work were done (p.28)?

options.text: Added new unit tests for new classes, added new functional tests for new functionality, updated impacted old tests
Deleted all old tests and wrote none

questions.explanation: Actualization added new unit tests for OwnerIdentity/SimpleListener, added new functional tests for owner enforcement, and updated the old tests impacted during propagation. It did not delete-all, only-baseline, or only-record. (Drawlets p.28)

questions.stem: In test-suite maintenance after the change (p.29), what happens to old tests not affected by the change?

options.text: They are converted to functional tests
They are kept as regression tests
They are merged into one test

questions.explanation: Unaffected old tests are kept as regression tests; obsolete tests are removed; new-feature tests are added. They are not deleted (that is the obsolete ones), converted, or merged. (Drawlets p.29)

questions.stem: What happens to obsolete tests during test-suite maintenance?

options.text: They are kept as regression tests
They are pulled up into ConstructionTool

questions.explanation: Obsolete tests are removed; unaffected ones become regression tests; new-feature tests are added. Obsolete tests are not kept, repurposed as acceptance tests, or moved into a class. (Drawlets p.29)

questions.stem: The guide calls test-suite maintenance the 'test-side analogue' of which production-code activity?
questions.explanation: Updating impacted tests, adding tests for new classes/features, keeping unaffected ones as regression, and deleting obsolete ones mirrors change propagation on the test side. It is not concept location, acceptance testing, or frequency tuning. (Drawlets p.28-29)

questions.stem: What is the Drawlets PRODUCTION-code baseline size?
questions.explanation: The production baseline is 17 800 LOC; 4 800 is the unit-test baseline, 40 000 is the whole framework figure, and 91 is the production LOC modified. (Drawlets p.30)

questions.stem: How many production LOC did the owner change modify, and what percentage of the production baseline is that?
questions.explanation: The change modified 91 production LOC (0.5 % of 17 800). 124 LOC / 2.5 % is the TEST-code figure; 17 800 is the whole baseline; 385 is a test count. (Drawlets p.30)

questions.stem: What is the approximate ratio of modified test LOC to modified production LOC for the owner change?
questions.explanation: 124 modified test LOC over 91 modified production LOC is about 1.4 test-LOC per production-LOC. The other ratios do not match the 124/91 figures. (Drawlets p.30)

questions.stem: In the comparison table, how many classes are MODIFIED with NO refactoring?
questions.explanation: No refactoring modifies 13 classes. 8 is move-function, 5 is splitting, and 2 is the classes-added (constant across all three). (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: In the comparison table, how many classes are MODIFIED with the 'move function' strategy?
questions.explanation: Move-function modifies 8 classes (down from 13 with no refactoring). 5 is splitting, and 2 is classes-added. (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: In the comparison table, how many classes are MODIFIED with the 'splitting' strategy?
questions.explanation: Splitting modifies just 5 classes - the fewest. 8 is move-function, 13 is no-refactoring, and 2 is classes-added. (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: Across the three strategies, the classes-modified count goes 13 -> 8 -> 5. What does this trend demonstrate?

options.text: Refactoring roughly halves the number of classes touched (shortens propagation)
Refactoring roughly doubles the lines edited
Refactoring removes the need for tests

questions.explanation: 13 -> 8 -> 5 shows refactoring roughly halving classes touched, i.e. shortening propagation - the real cost. It does not double lines, remove tests, or increase the blast radius. (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: Across the three strategies, LOC modified is 91 -> 95 -> 87. What does this near-constant figure show?

options.text: Refactoring barely changes the lines edited, even as classes-touched drops sharply
LOC and classes-modified always move together
Refactoring drastically cuts the lines edited

questions.explanation: LOC stays near constant (91/95/87) while classes-modified drops 13->8->5, showing the lines typed are NOT the real cost - propagation (classes) is. LOC does not drop drastically, does not track classes, and splitting actually edits the fewest lines (87). (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: Which strategy yields the fewest classes modified AND the fewest LOC modified?
questions.explanation: Splitting has both the fewest classes (5) and the fewest LOC (87). No-refactoring is 13/91, move-function is 8/95, and they are not equal. (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: Splitting modifies the fewest classes (5). Why does the deck warn this is NOT automatically the least total effort?

options.text: A new set of unit tests must be created (test code is duplicated), and writing new tests costs more than adapting existing ones
Splitting modifies the most production LOC
Splitting adds the most new classes

questions.explanation: The splitting minuses: a new set of unit tests must be created because test code is duplicated, and creating new tests costs more than adapting existing ones - so fewest-classes is not automatically least-effort. Splitting does not edit the most LOC, add the most classes, or re-run concept location. (Drawlets p.38)

questions.stem: What is the deck's overall conclusion (p.38) about which refactoring may require the least WORK?

options.text: Splitting a method into two similar methods may require the least work, with the caveat of duplicated test code

questions.explanation: The deck concludes splitting may require the least work, while warning of duplicated test code and higher new-test cost. No-refactoring (13 classes) is not cheapest, move-function is intermediate, and refactoring does pay off in propagation length. (Drawlets p.38)

questions.stem: What is the lecture's central thesis about the cost of a change?

options.text: A change's cost equals the number of unit tests
A change's real cost is the size of the propagation it triggers, not the number of lines finally typed
A change's cost equals its LOC modified
A change's cost is fixed regardless of structure

questions.explanation: The decisive lesson: cost = propagation size, not edit size - 0.5 % of LOC rippled through ~13 classes, and refactoring (not fewer lines) shortened it. Cost is not LOC, not test count, and is very much affected by structure. (Drawlets p.30, p.37)

questions.stem: Comparing 'no refactoring' and 'splitting': classes-modified drops 13->5 but LOC drops only 91->87. What does this reveal about where refactoring delivers its benefit?

options.text: The benefit is more new classes added
The benefit is in shortened propagation (fewer classes), not in fewer lines typed
The benefit is mainly fewer lines typed

questions.explanation: The big drop is in classes touched (13->5) while LOC barely moves (91->87), so refactoring's payoff is shorter propagation, not fewer lines. It is not mainly about lines, it is measurable, and classes-added stays at 2. (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: Through how many phases does the Drawlets worked example trace the owner change?
questions.explanation: The worked example traces the change through all eight phases (Initiation -> Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Conclusion -> Verification). Three is the conclusion's steps, not phases. (Drawlets, whole deck)

questions.stem: Which ordering of the eight SB5-MAI phases does the worked example follow?

options.text: Impact Analysis, Concept Location, Prefactoring, Initiation, Conclusion, Verification
Concept Location, Initiation, Actualization, Impact Analysis, Verification, Conclusion
Initiation, Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring, Actualization, Postfactoring, Conclusion, Verification
Actualization, Prefactoring, Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Conclusion, Verification

questions.explanation: The phases run Initiation -> Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Conclusion -> Verification (the last wrapping all). The other orderings scramble the spine. (Drawlets, Phase headings)

questions.stem: Which phase is described as a vertical band wrapping every other phase rather than a single step?
questions.explanation: Verification is the vertical band that wraps every phase (it is concretely the test suite). Conclusion, Concept Location, and Actualization are discrete steps in the spine. (Conclusion p.1, Drawlets Phase 8)

questions.stem: Map the owner-change events to phases: 'locate AbstractFigure', 'introduce OwnerIdentity/SimpleListener', 'split move/secureMove', 'certify the new baseline'. Which mapping is correct?

options.text: Impact Analysis, Concept Location, Prefactoring, Verification

questions.explanation: Locating AbstractFigure = Concept Location; introducing the two new classes = Actualization; splitting move/secureMove = Postfactoring; certifying the baseline = Conclusion. The other mappings misassign at least one event. (Drawlets p.15, p.16, p.36; Conclusion p.3)

questions.stem: Which event belongs to Postfactoring rather than Actualization in the worked example?

options.text: Adding OwnerIdentity to carry the user identity
Marking AbstractFigure red and propagating the owner concept
Splitting AbstractFigure.move into move/secureMove to shorten future propagation
Adding SimpleListener as the Observer wiring

questions.explanation: Splitting move/secureMove is a refactoring AFTER the change works - Postfactoring. Adding OwnerIdentity/SimpleListener and propagating from AbstractFigure are Actualization. (Drawlets p.16 vs p.36)

questions.stem: Which event belongs to Concept Location rather than Impact Analysis?

options.text: Estimating that the ripple reaches the whole upper graph
Backtracking from SimpleDrawingCanvas to find AbstractFigure
Identifying that tools and containers will be affected

questions.explanation: Backtracking to find AbstractFigure is Concept Location (finding WHERE the concept lives). Estimating the ripple/whole-graph/~13-classes/tools-and-containers are Impact Analysis (estimating WHAT will be touched). (Drawlets p.10-15 vs p.16)

questions.stem: The 'move the code' refactoring (Extract + Pull Up Method) is attributed to which reading?
questions.explanation: Extract Method and Pull Up Method are Fowler's refactorings [Fowler99]. Rajlich supplies the example, GoF supplies patterns, and Martin supplies Clean Code/Architecture. (Drawlets p.33, citation key)

questions.stem: The Observer/listener structure (e.g. the new SimpleListener) is a pattern from which reading?
questions.explanation: Observer is a Gang of Four design pattern [GHJV94]; SimpleListener is its instance in the change. Fowler covers refactoring, Rajlich the example, and Martin architecture. (Drawlets p.16, citation key)

questions.stem: Which is true of the relationship between commit and baseline?

options.text: Commit certifies the whole system; baseline returns code to one developer
Commit returns code to the team's repository; baseline certifies the integrated whole for the project
Commit and baseline are the same action

questions.explanation: Commit returns code to the team repo; baseline certifies the integrated whole. The 'commit certifies / baseline returns to one developer' option inverts them, 'same action' conflates them, and 'baseline before commit' reverses the order (you commit, then the committed code is baselined). (Conclusion p.2-3)

questions.stem: How does the 'bugs in baseline' decision relate to technical debt?

options.text: It has no relationship to technical debt
It converts bugs into new features
Deferred minor-bug fixes are logged on the bug stack as debt to be paid in future changes

questions.explanation: Certifying despite minor bugs preserves progress but logs debt: the fixes go on the bug-report stack for future changes. It does not eliminate debt, convert bugs to features, or stand apart from debt. (Conclusion p.6)

questions.stem: A change request passes baseline testing but stakeholders refuse it at acceptance testing. Which gate failed, and what does it block?

options.text: Concept location failed; it blocks impact analysis
The internal baseline gate failed; it blocks commit
The deadline was missed; it blocks the current baseline
The external acceptance gate failed; it blocks release to users

questions.explanation: Passing baseline but failing acceptance means the external stakeholder gate failed, blocking release - the internal certification already succeeded. It is not a baseline/commit failure, not concept location, and not a missed deadline. (Conclusion p.8)

questions.stem: Why does the guide call the Drawlets propagation 'the heart of the lecture'?

options.text: It demonstrates how to write unit tests
It concretely shows one small edit rippling through a large part of the class graph, the lecture's core point
It lists all design patterns in Drawlets

questions.explanation: The propagation animation makes the lecture's core point concrete: a tiny edit ripples widely, so structure (and refactoring) matters. It is not primarily about writing tests, cataloguing patterns, or the merge tool. (Drawlets p.16-24)

questions.stem: Which pairing of search states to Drawlets colours is correct for concept location?

options.text: green = comprehended, orange = currently-examined, red = located concept, grey = backtracked/abandoned
green = new class, red = test, blue = deleted, grey = released
orange = released, red = committed, green = baselined, grey = patched
red = comprehended, green = located, grey = frontier, blue = obsolete

questions.explanation: In concept location: green = comprehended, orange = currently-examined on the fly, red = the located concept/class needing change, grey = backtracked/dead-end (blue boxes are new classes). The other mappings are invented. (Drawlets grounding note, p.9-15)

questions.stem: Why does the deck animate change propagation with spreading marks rather than a static list?

options.text: To show that a changed class makes neighbours inconsistent, spreading the wave until consistency is restored
Because the marks are required by the merge tool
Because colours determine the baseline deadline

questions.explanation: The spreading marks visualise the wave: one change makes neighbours inconsistent, you visit each, possibly spreading further, until no inconsistencies remain. The colours are pedagogical, not merge-tool, deadline, or stakeholder artefacts. (Drawlets p.16-24)

questions.stem: Which single statement best summarises how Half 1 (Conclusion) and Half 2 (Drawlets) of the lecture fit together?

options.text: Half 1 tells you how a change ends; Half 2 shows every phase that came before the end, made concrete
Half 1 is the worked example; Half 2 is the theory
Both halves describe only the conclusion phase
They are unrelated topics taught together by accident

questions.explanation: Read together, Half 1 (Conclusion) explains how a change ends, and Half 2 (Drawlets) makes concrete every phase leading to that end. They are not both conclusion-only, the halves are not swapped, and they are deliberately complementary. (Guide overview)

questions.stem: What is the difference between the blue boxes and the red classes in the actualization/propagation diagrams?

options.text: Blue boxes are deleted classes; red are external concepts
Blue boxes are the frontier; red are unchanged
Blue boxes are tests; red are interfaces
Blue boxes are the new classes added; red classes are existing classes that changed or are inconsistent

questions.explanation: Blue boxes = the two new classes (OwnerIdentity, SimpleListener); red = existing classes changed/inconsistent. Blue are not deletions/tests/frontier, and red are not unchanged or external. (Drawlets p.16-24)

questions.stem: Impact analysis predicted the ripple BEFORE coding; the comparison table reports classes-modified AFTER coding. Which best states their relationship?

options.text: Impact analysis is the up-front estimate; the table's 13/8/5 are the realised counts confirming (and refinable by refactoring) that estimate
Impact analysis happens after the table is filled in
The table is computed during concept location

questions.explanation: Impact analysis estimates the blast radius up front; the realised 13/8/5 classes-modified confirm it (and show refactoring can shrink it). They are not unrelated, the table is not concept-location output, and impact analysis precedes coding/the table. (Drawlets p.16, p.37)

questions.stem: What certifies that committed code has become the team's new agreed reference version?
questions.explanation: When committed-and-integrated code passes baseline testing, it becomes the team's new agreed reference version. One developer's approval, a release download, and a refactoring do not certify a baseline. (Conclusion p.2-3)

questions.stem: Why are unaffected old tests kept rather than discarded after a change?

options.text: Because the merge tool requires them
To serve as regression tests guarding future changes

questions.explanation: Unaffected tests are kept as regression tests to guard future changes; obsolete ones are removed and new-feature ones added. They are not kept for the merge tool, stakeholder authorship, or obsolescence (the opposite). (Drawlets p.29)

questions.stem: How does the Drawlets functional testing connect to the conclusion's acceptance step?

options.text: Functional tests are run only after release
Functional tests replace the baseline test entirely
Acceptance testing is done by the CI server alone
JGiven+Mockito functional tests feed the stakeholders' acceptance decision (BDD-style verification)

questions.explanation: The JGiven+Mockito functional tests (plus record/playback) are the BDD-style verification feeding stakeholders' acceptance decision. They do not replace baseline testing, are not CI-only, and are not post-release. (Drawlets p.27; Conclusion p.8)

questions.stem: In the role-splitting refactoring, what does 'propagating the change highlights the difference' mean?

options.text: It means the merge tool flags a conflict
It means the change must be propagated twice
Working the change reveals that one method actually serves two distinct roles, only one of which needs the owner check
It means the difference is in the test code

questions.explanation: Propagating the owner change exposes that move() plays two roles (user-requested vs creation-time), and only the user-requested one needs the identity check - enabling the split. It is not double propagation, a test-code difference, or a merge conflict. (Drawlets p.35)

questions.stem: If an exam asks you to 'trace a change through JHotDraw', why is the Drawlets walkthrough the template to reproduce?

options.text: JHotDraw and Drawlets share the same source code
Drawlets is a unit test of JHotDraw
Both share the Figure/canvas/tool graph and the concept-location-then-propagation rhythm
JHotDraw has no figures or tools

questions.explanation: Drawlets and JHotDraw share the same Figure/canvas/tool structure and the same concept-location-then-propagation rhythm, so the Drawlets trace transfers directly. They are not the same source, not test-of, and JHotDraw does have figures/tools. (Drawlets, JHotDraw connection)

questions.stem: What does the merge tool do in the small-release path?

options.text: Incorporates a patch into the user's program

questions.explanation: The 'merge' tool incorporates a small-release patch into the user's program. It does not certify baselines, classify concepts, or run unit tests. (Conclusion p.10)

questions.stem: Given splitting touches the fewest classes (5) yet the deck still flags a test-cost downside, which reasoning is correct?

options.text: Splitting deletes the need for functional tests
New methods need a new (duplicated) set of unit tests, and writing new tests costs more than adapting existing ones, so total effort may exceed move-function
Test cost is independent of the refactoring chosen
Fewer classes always means fewer tests, so splitting is cheapest overall

questions.explanation: Splitting creates new methods that require a new, duplicated set of unit tests, and new tests cost more to write than adapting existing ones - so fewest-classes need not be least-effort. Fewer classes does not guarantee fewer/cheaper tests, functional tests are still needed, and test cost depends on the refactoring. (Drawlets p.38)

questions.stem: Which statement about acceptance testing's place in the layering is correct?

options.text: It is identical to unit testing
It is external (stakeholders) and follows the internal baseline certification
It is internal (testing team) and precedes commit

questions.explanation: Acceptance testing is external, done by stakeholders, and follows the internal baseline certification. It is not internal, does not precede commit, does not replace merge, and differs from unit testing. (Conclusion p.8)

questions.stem: On p.11/p.14 the abandoned classes (e.g. ToolPalette, SimpleDrawingCanvas) go grey. What does grey mean in the concept-location colour code?

options.text: Backtracked / dead-end, abandoned for this search

questions.explanation: In concept location, grey marks backtracked/dead-end classes abandoned for this search. Red is the located concept, orange the frontier, and blue a new class. (Drawlets p.11, p.14)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say commit, baseline, and release each represent 'a higher bar'?

options.text: Each step adds more lines of code
Each step exposes the change to a wider audience with stricter acceptance: team, then project, then users

questions.explanation: Commit (team) -> baseline (project) -> release (users) widens the audience and raises the acceptance bar at each step. The steps do not add LOC, remove tests, or shorten propagation. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: What does 'Propagation - done' (p.24) signify about the system's internal state?

options.text: The system has been released to users
The system is internally consistent again with owners enforced

questions.explanation: At Propagation-done the system is internally consistent again with owners enforced - no inconsistencies remain. It does not mean release, test deletion, or restarting concept location. (Drawlets p.24)

questions.stem: Acceptance testing gives stakeholders what, in addition to being a release gate?
questions.explanation: Acceptance testing gives stakeholders information about the project's progress and is the gate to approve release. It does not hand them source to edit, commit access, or the deadline. (Conclusion p.8)

questions.stem: Which scenario describes a missed deadline rather than a broken baseline?

options.text: No new baseline is created because of defects
The testing team rejects buggy commits and invalidates the increment
Serious bugs cause reputations to suffer
A programmer's correct work simply was not committed before baseline testing started

questions.explanation: Not committing correct work in time is a missed deadline (timing), distinct from a broken baseline (severity: rejected commits, invalidated increment, reputation harm). The other three describe a broken baseline. (Conclusion p.5 vs p.7)

questions.stem: Drawlets is an application framework that does what for its host application?

options.text: Adds a graphical drawing display/canvas with toolbars and tool buttons supplied by the host

questions.explanation: Drawlets adds a graphical drawing display to a host application; the host supplies the canvas, toolbars and tool buttons (the bundled host is SimpleApplet). It is not a database, compiler, or CI runner. (Drawlets p.1, p.3)

questions.stem: During propagation, when a class is visited and found to need no change, what is done to it?

options.text: It is released as a patch
It is converted into a new class
It is deleted from the system
It is confirmed consistent (greyed) and the wave continues elsewhere

questions.explanation: A visited-but-unaffected class is confirmed consistent (greyed) and the wave continues; it is not deleted, converted, or released. (Drawlets p.16-24)

questions.stem: Between move-function (8 classes) and splitting (5 classes), why might move-function nonetheless be preferable in practice?

options.text: Move-function modifies fewer LOC than splitting
Move-function touches fewer classes than splitting
Splitting duplicates test code and new tests cost more to write, so move-function's adapt-existing-tests effort can be lower despite touching more classes

questions.explanation: Although splitting touches fewer classes (5 vs 8), it duplicates test code and new tests cost more than adapting existing ones, so move-function may be cheaper overall. Move-function touches MORE classes (8), both add 2 classes, and splitting actually edits fewer LOC (87 vs 95). (Drawlets p.37-38)

questions.stem: What hypothesis did the maintainer test (and reject) at SimpleDrawingCanvas, and why was it wrong?

options.text: That a figure's own properties live in its container; wrong because the container only holds figures, not their individual state
That the canvas is a new class; wrong because it already existed
That the canvas is external; wrong because it is significant
That the canvas owns no figures; wrong because it owns all of them

questions.explanation: The rejected hypothesis was that figure properties live in the container SimpleDrawingCanvas; it is wrong because the container only holds figures, while a single figure's own state (owner) belongs on AbstractFigure. The canvas is not new, not external, and does hold figures. (Drawlets p.10, p.13)

questions.stem: Which best describes the role of the configuration-management repository in the conclusion?

options.text: It is the user's installed program
It is the shared store the team commits updated code into, resolving conflicts
It is where stakeholders run acceptance tests

questions.explanation: The CM repository is the shared store programmers commit into (resolving conflicts). It is not the acceptance environment, the user's program, or the bug stack. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: Which combination correctly classifies the post-change fate of the three test categories?

options.text: Unaffected -> added; obsolete -> kept; new-feature -> removed
Unaffected -> regression; obsolete -> removed; new-feature -> added
Unaffected -> removed; obsolete -> regression; new-feature -> added

questions.explanation: Unaffected tests become regression tests, obsolete tests are removed, and new-feature tests are added. The other options swap or remove the wrong categories. (Drawlets p.29)

questions.stem: Why does the propagation reach SimpleApplet (turning it red) even though the located concept was AbstractFigure?

options.text: SimpleApplet is one of the two new classes
The owner feature needs session/ID management, which SimpleApplet provides, so the change reaches it through the dependency wave

questions.explanation: AbstractFigure is the origin, but the owner feature requires session/ID handling that SimpleApplet provides, so the propagation wave reaches and changes it (red). SimpleApplet is not the located concept (that was AbstractFigure), not a new class, and not unaffected. (Drawlets p.19)

questions.stem: Which is the correct definition of a 'patch' in the release context?

options.text: An obsolete test removed during maintenance
A rejected commit from a broken baseline
A small release incorporated into the user's program by the tool 'merge'
A large release downloaded and installed

questions.explanation: A patch is a small release merged into the user's program via 'merge'. A large release is downloaded/installed, a rejected commit comes from a broken baseline, and an obsolete test is a verification artefact. (Conclusion p.10)

questions.stem: Refactoring shortens change propagation primarily by reducing what?

options.text: The number of classes the change must touch

questions.explanation: Refactoring shortens propagation by reducing the number of classes touched (13 -> 8 -> 5), even though lines stay near constant. It is not about lines, stakeholders, or releases. (Drawlets p.31, p.37)

questions.stem: Why does the guide frame the closing Drawlets refactorings as illustrating prefactoring even though they are shown after the change?

options.text: Because they delete the change request
Because prefactoring always happens after actualization
They are exactly the clean-ups one would do BEFORE a future owner-related change to make it cheaper

questions.explanation: Shown after the change as analysis, the move-moveFigure and split-move/secureMove refactorings are precisely the prefactoring one would do before a future owner-related change to shorten its propagation. Prefactoring does not follow actualization, delete requests, or constitute acceptance tests. (Drawlets p.31-36, Phase 4)

questions.stem: Across the conclusion, which ordering of audiences correctly reflects increasing exposure?
questions.explanation: Exposure widens team -> project/testing team -> stakeholders -> users. The other orderings scramble the increasing-exposure sequence. (Conclusion p.2, p.8, p.9)

questions.stem: The owner change added new unit tests for which classes specifically?

options.text: The new classes OwnerIdentity and SimpleListener
No new unit tests were added

questions.explanation: Actualization added new unit tests for the new classes OwnerIdentity and SimpleListener (plus new functional tests for owner enforcement). The leaf tools/palettes were checked during propagation, and new tests certainly were added. (Drawlets p.28)

questions.stem: Which of these is a figure type listed for Drawlets?
questions.explanation: Drawlets supports lines, freehand, rectangles, rounded rectangles, triangles, pentagons, polygons, ellipses, text boxes and images. Pie charts, bar graphs and histograms are not in its figure list. (Drawlets p.1)

questions.stem: An exam answer claims 'the conclusion phase is finished once the code is committed and the baseline passes.' What is missing for the change to truly reach users?

options.text: Nothing; commit plus baseline reaches users
A second round of concept location
Stakeholder acceptance testing and the actual release (large install or merged patch)

questions.explanation: Commit plus baseline certifies the code internally, but reaching users still requires stakeholder acceptance and the actual release (download/install or merged patch). Concept location and impact analysis are earlier phases, not missing conclusion steps. (Conclusion p.8-10)

questions.stem: Impact analysis on the owner change concluded it was which kind of change?

options.text: A non-trivial, cross-cutting change touching much of the upper graph
A change requiring no new classes
A change confined to the test suite

questions.explanation: Impact analysis flagged a non-trivial, cross-cutting change reaching much of the upper graph (later confirmed: ~13 classes, 2 new). It is not a one-class edit, test-only, or class-free. (Drawlets p.16, p.37)

questions.stem: Which row of the comparison table is INVARIANT across all three strategies?

options.text: Both classes modified and LOC modified

questions.explanation: Classes added (2) and interfaces modified (1) are constant across all three strategies; classes modified (13/8/5) and LOC modified (91/95/87) vary. (Drawlets p.37)

questions.stem: What is the very first thing that must exist for Initiation to occur?

options.text: A change request deemed worth doing

questions.explanation: Initiation means a change request exists and is deemed worth doing. A passing baseline, merged patch, and located concept all come later in the process. (Drawlets p.6)

questions.stem: Which describes the correct anchor-and-direction for the owner concept location?

options.text: Anchor on 'owner' and follow DOWN to the canvas
Anchor on the significant concept 'figure' and follow dependencies UP to the abstraction AbstractFigure
Anchor on 'password' and search the palettes
Anchor on 'canvas' and stop at SimpleDrawingCanvas

questions.explanation: You anchor on the significant existing concept figure and go UP to the abstraction AbstractFigure (where per-figure state lives). Anchoring on the external owner/password, or stopping at the container canvas, is the wrong-way pattern. (Drawlets p.8, p.12, p.15)
