questions.stem: Which single sentence best states the lecture's punchline about structure and change cost?

options.text: The number of lines typed equals the cost of a change
A change touching ~0.5 % of code can ripple through much of the class graph, so good structure (or refactoring) is what keeps the ripple short
Refactoring increases the cost of future changes

questions.explanation: The punchline: a 0.5 %-of-LOC edit still rippled through a large part of the class graph, and structure/refactoring is what keeps the ripple (the real cost) short. Cost is not lines typed, structured code still propagates (just less), and refactoring lowers future cost. (Guide overview; Drawlets p.30, p.37)

questions.stem: What does the ~1.4 test-LOC-per-production-LOC figure tell a maintainer to expect?

options.text: Tests are exactly equal in size to production
For every production line changed, roughly 1.4 test lines change too
Production code changes 1.4x more than test code
Test code never changes during a change

questions.explanation: About 1.4 test lines change per modified production line (124/91), so a maintainer should budget more test changes than production changes. Test code does change, it changes MORE not less, and the suites are not equal in size (4 800 vs 17 800 baseline). (Drawlets p.30)

questions.stem: Which is the correct definition of 'broken baseline'?

options.text: Minor bugs that are certified anyway and deferred to the bug stack
Serious bugs cause the testing team to reject commits; the whole increment may be invalidated/postponed
A patch that fails to merge

questions.explanation: A broken baseline = serious bugs, rejected commits, possibly invalidated/postponed increment. Minor-and-certified is bugs-in-baseline, a missed deadline is timing, and a failed merge is a delivery issue. (Conclusion p.7)

questions.stem: Which Fowler refactoring name matches 'raise a method from subclasses into their common base class'?
questions.explanation: Raising a method into the common base class is Pull Up Method (used to move moveFigure into ConstructionTool). Extract Method pulls logic out into a new method, Inline does the reverse of Extract, and Rename only renames. (Drawlets p.33)

questions.stem: Which Fowler refactoring name matches 'turn a fragment of code into its own method'?
questions.explanation: Turning a code fragment into its own method is Extract Method (used to create moveFigure from basicNewFigure). Pull Up moves a method to the base class, and the other two are unrelated refactorings. (Drawlets p.33)

questions.stem: Why is the baseline both a technical and a social mechanism?

options.text: Technically it synchronises integrated code; socially the deadline is visible to management who track misses
It is neither technical nor social, only financial
It is purely social with no technical aspect
It is purely technical with no social aspect

questions.explanation: The baseline synchronises integrated code (technical) while its deadline is visible to management who track misses and may demand explanations (social). It is not purely technical, purely social, or merely financial. (Conclusion p.5)

questions.stem: On 'Propagation - 4' (p.21), the wave reaches which region of the class graph?

options.text: The Locator/LocatorConnectionHandle region and the tool subtree

questions.explanation: At p.21 the wave reaches the Locator/LocatorConnectionHandle region and the tool subtree (driving toward ConstructionTool and LabelTool). The bug stack, installer, and acceptance scripts are not nodes in the propagation graph. (Drawlets p.21)

questions.stem: Which statement correctly orders the FOUR distinct quality/coordination gates a finished change passes in the conclusion?

options.text: Commit (resolve conflicts) -> baseline testing (testing team) -> acceptance testing (stakeholders) -> release (users)

questions.explanation: The gates run commit -> baseline testing -> acceptance testing -> release, widening scope each time. The other orderings misplace at least one gate. (Conclusion p.2, p.3, p.8, p.9)

questions.stem: What does 'commit' hand the code to, in the three-audience model?
questions.explanation: Commit hands code to the team (via the CM repository). Users get it at release, stakeholders at acceptance, and the last option is nonsensical. (Conclusion p.2)

questions.stem: What problem does a 'long change propagation' represent?

options.text: A change touches many classes, making it (and future similar changes) costly
The change request is too small

questions.explanation: A long propagation means the change ripples through many classes, raising its cost and that of future similar changes - which is why refactoring to shorten it is advantageous. It is not about too-few classes, no dependencies, or a small request. (Drawlets p.31)

questions.stem: Verification in the worked example is concretely embodied by what?

options.text: The test suite (385 unit tests + 141 functional tests, run via JGiven/Mockito)

questions.explanation: Verification is concretely the test suite: 385 unit tests, 141 functional tests, run with JGiven/Mockito and a record/playback tool. The merge tool, classification table, and installer are not verification. (Drawlets p.25-27)

questions.stem: Why does the deck say the conclusion's activities 'depend on the specific software process' rather than prescribing one workflow?

options.text: Different processes (e.g. CI vs quarterly releases) commit, baseline, and release on different cadences and with different tooling

questions.explanation: The conclusion's concrete form varies because CI shops and quarterly-release shops differ in cadence/tooling, though the same three logical steps occur. The conclusion is well-defined, many processes exist, and stakeholders do not forbid workflows. (Conclusion p.1)

questions.stem: Why is concept location described as 'search, not lookup' specifically in the Drawlets example?

options.text: A wrong hypothesis (canvas/container) had to be tested and backtracked before the right class (AbstractFigure) was found
The search ran the unit tests to find the class
No searching was needed; AbstractFigure was obvious
The class was found by a simple keyword search

questions.explanation: It is search-not-lookup because the maintainer formed a hypothesis (properties in the container), tested it, hit a dead end, backtracked, and only then found AbstractFigure. It was not a keyword search, not obvious, and did not use unit tests to locate the class. (Drawlets p.9-15)

questions.stem: Which two outcomes can baseline testing produce when committed code is faulty?

options.text: Bugs-in-baseline (certify + defer) or broken-baseline (reject + possibly invalidate)

questions.explanation: Faulty code yields either bugs-in-baseline (still certify, defer fixes) or broken-baseline (reject commits, maybe invalidate the increment). The other pairs are unrelated process steps or delivery formats. (Conclusion p.6-7)

questions.stem: What does the move-the-code refactoring do to the duplicated 'move it if at the wrong location' logic?

options.text: Leaves it duplicated in every creation tool
Extracts it into moveFigure(...) and pulls it up so it lives once in ConstructionTool
Moves it into the SimpleListener Observer

questions.explanation: The duplicated move logic is extracted into moveFigure(...) and pulled up into ConstructionTool, so it lives once. It is not left duplicated, moved to the listener, or deleted. (Drawlets p.33)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say verification 'wraps every phase' rather than being a single late step?

options.text: Tests are written/updated throughout (during actualization propagation and after) and the verification band spans all phases
Verification is identical to acceptance testing

questions.explanation: Verification is the vertical band spanning all phases; tests are updated during propagation, added for new classes/features, and maintained throughout. It is not post-release-only, not identical to acceptance, and does not replace baseline testing. (Conclusion p.1; Drawlets p.28-29)

questions.stem: Reading the propagation sequence p.17->p.24, which best describes how the 'frontier' behaves?

options.text: It moves only among the two new classes
It stays fixed on AbstractFigure the whole time
It jumps randomly between unconnected classes
It starts at AbstractFigure/Figure and sweeps outward through canvas, host, palettes, and the tool subtree to the leaf tools, each node changed or confirmed

questions.explanation: The frontier starts at the figure abstraction and sweeps outward through the canvas, SimpleApplet, palettes, and the tool subtree down to the leaf creation tools, marking each changed or confirmed. It is not random, not confined to new classes, and does not stay fixed. (Drawlets p.17-24)

questions.stem: The conclusion phase sits below which phase in the change-process diagram?
questions.explanation: Conclusion sits below Postfactoring in the phase diagram (still spanned by the Verification band). Initiation, Concept Location, and Impact Analysis are all earlier/higher phases. (Conclusion p.1)

questions.stem: Suppose the owner change's commits pass baseline testing with only a couple of minor cosmetic bugs. Per the deck, what is the most likely testing-team decision and its ledger effect?

options.text: Reject all commits and invalidate the whole increment
Refuse certification until cosmetic bugs are fixed in this baseline
Certify the baseline and add the cosmetic fixes to the bug-report stack for a future change

questions.explanation: Minor cosmetic bugs are a bugs-in-baseline case: certify and defer the fixes to the bug stack. Rejecting/invalidating is for serious (broken) defects; refusing certification for cosmetic issues contradicts the deck; and release never bypasses acceptance. (Conclusion p.6)

questions.stem: Which best characterises the Drawlets functional tests as a group?

options.text: Pure unit tests with no scenarios
Scenario-style cases like Draw/Select/Move covering figure operations, 141 in total

questions.explanation: Functional tests are 141 scenario-style cases (Draw, Select, Move, ...) covering figure operations. They are not unit tests, benchmarks, or type checks. (Drawlets p.26)

questions.stem: Which is the correct cause->effect for a missed baseline deadline?

options.text: Work excluded from this baseline -> must re-submit next time -> extra re-integration work against a moved baseline

questions.explanation: Missing the deadline excludes the work from this baseline, forcing re-submission next time with extra re-integration work against a moved baseline. Auto-merge, increment invalidation, and rejected commits describe other situations (the latter two are broken-baseline). (Conclusion p.5)

questions.stem: Why does the deck's final conclusion stop short of declaring 'splitting' the universally best refactoring despite its 5-class minimum?

options.text: Because splitting modifies the most classes
Because new methods force duplicated, newly-written unit tests, so total effort (including test cost) can exceed the other strategies

questions.explanation: Splitting touches the fewest classes but creates new methods that require a fresh, duplicated set of unit tests - and new tests cost more than adapting existing ones - so once test cost is counted it may not be best. Splitting touches the FEWEST classes (5), still enforces ownership, and adds the same 1 interface. (Drawlets p.37-38)

questions.stem: What is the relationship between 'new baseline' and 'new release' in the worked example's conclusion?

options.text: The baseline is certified internally first; only from time to time is a release delivered to users
Release and baseline are the same activity

questions.explanation: The baseline is certified internally, and from time to time the baseline is delivered to users as a release. Release does not precede the baseline, not every baseline is released, and they are distinct activities. (Conclusion p.2, p.9)

questions.stem: What is the purpose of starting concept location from an already-comprehended class like SimpleApplet?

options.text: To have a known foothold from which to follow dependencies toward the target concept
Because SimpleApplet is the canvas container
Because SimpleApplet is a new class
Because SimpleApplet holds the owner property

questions.explanation: You start from a comprehended class (SimpleApplet, the host) as a foothold to follow dependencies toward where 'figure properties' live. SimpleApplet does not hold the owner property (AbstractFigure does), is not new, and is not the canvas (that is SimpleDrawingCanvas). (Drawlets p.9)

questions.stem: Which tool pair is named for running Drawlets' functional/acceptance tests?

options.text: JGiven and Mockito (with a record/playback tool)

questions.explanation: JGiven and Mockito (plus a record/playback tool) run Drawlets' functional/acceptance tests. JUnit/Hamcrest, TestNG/Spock, and Cypress/Jest are not the named tools. (Drawlets p.27)

questions.stem: Which statement about release cadence is most accurate per the deck?

options.text: Patches and large releases are delivered identically
Teams commonly mix infrequent large releases with frequent small releases (patches)
Teams release the entire baseline every commit
Teams only ever ship one final release

questions.explanation: The common pattern mixes infrequent large releases with frequent small patches (versioned model). Not every commit ships, not one final release, and patches (merged) differ from large releases (downloaded/installed). (Conclusion p.9-10)

questions.stem: What colour marks the located origin class AbstractFigure when actualization begins?
questions.explanation: AbstractFigure is marked red as the origin gaining the owner concept. Grey = checked-unchanged, green = reconciled, blue = a new class. (Drawlets p.16)

questions.stem: The role-splitting refactoring is motivated by the realisation that one method serves how many roles?
questions.explanation: Role-splitting is motivated by one method serving TWO roles (user-requested move vs creation-time move), only one of which needs the owner check. Not one, three, or zero. (Drawlets p.35)

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly describes the increasing standard of approval through commit -> baseline -> acceptance -> release?

options.text: user delivery -> stakeholder approval -> internal certification -> team integration
stakeholder approval -> team integration -> user delivery -> internal certification
team integration -> internal certification -> stakeholder approval -> user delivery
internal certification -> user delivery -> team integration -> stakeholder approval

questions.explanation: The standard rises: team integration (commit) -> internal certification (baseline) -> stakeholder approval (acceptance) -> user delivery (release). The other orderings scramble this. (Conclusion p.2, p.3, p.8, p.9)

questions.stem: Impact analysis is best summarised as estimating what before any code is written?

options.text: How many users will download the release
Which classes the change will touch (its scope/blast radius)

questions.explanation: Impact analysis estimates the change's scope/blast radius before coding. User downloads, obsolete tests, and exact final LOC are determined later, not by impact analysis. (Drawlets p.16, Phase 3)

questions.stem: A team uses continuous integration. Per the deck, how does this change the conclusion compared with a quarterly-release shop?

options.text: CI merges concept location into release
CI removes the need for a baseline
The cadence and tooling of commit/baseline/release differ, but the same three logical steps still occur

questions.explanation: Under CI the cadence/tooling differ (frequent integration), yet the same three logical steps (commit, baseline, release) still occur. CI does not remove the baseline, eliminate commit, or merge concept location into release. (Conclusion p.1)

questions.stem: Across the three refactoring strategies, why does the test cost differ even when classes-modified drops?

options.text: Tests are identical regardless of strategy
No refactoring writes the most tests
Splitting creates new methods needing newly written (duplicated) tests, costing more than adapting existing tests in the other strategies

questions.explanation: Splitting's new methods need new, duplicated tests (costlier than adapting), so fewer classes modified does not mean lower test cost. Tests are not identical across strategies, move-function does not delete tests, and the test-cost warning is specifically about splitting. (Drawlets p.38)

questions.stem: Delivering baseline code to actual users is which conclusion step?
questions.explanation: Delivering baseline code to users is the new-release step. Commit returns code to the team, baseline certifies it, and acceptance is the stakeholder gate. (Conclusion p.2, p.9)
